environment.hardware: No special hardware required. The TFIM demo operates on 2^7=128-dimensional dense complex matrices (7-site open chain) via a hand-rolled Taylor-series/scaling-and-squaring matrix exponential and power iteration; verified on Apple Silicon (aarch64) macOS 25.5.0 (Darwin), no GPU/cluster/large-memory dependency. The LaTeX build is a standard single-machine pdflatex+bibtex compile with no figure/asset dependencies (only tikz-cd commutative diagrams and a tikzpicture DOI sidebar, both from already-declared packages).
environment.software: GHC 9.14.1 (Homebrew) for the Haskell suite; pdfTeX 3.141592653-2.6-1.40.29 (TeX Live 2026) with BibTeX 0.99e for the paper build.
dependencies: base >=4 && <5 (GHC boot package; no version bound stated anywhere since no .cabal/stack.yaml exists for this paper's code)
QuickCheck 2.18.0.0 (imported by Properties.hs; not declared in any manifest anywhere in the repository -- see concerns for the machine-state dependency this creates)

concerns: No .cabal file, stack.yaml, or README exists for src/lieb-robinson-locality/ (bare FFunction.hs, Ising.hs, LinAlg.hs, Main.hs, Properties.hs), unlike sibling topic lattice-eft-equivalence which ships a .cabal manifest declaring its QuickCheck dependency. This is not merely cosmetic: Section 9 (line ~1088) states the reported numbers are 'reproducible from a single ghc build,' but Properties.hs imports Test.QuickCheck, which is not a GHC boot package (absent from `ghc-pkg list`'s global database). I directly tested the literal claim by running `GHC_ENVIRONMENT=- ghc --make -Wall -Wextra -Werror Main.hs` (an environment-variable override that makes GHC ignore this machine's ambient package-environment file, without modifying any shared files) and it fails immediately with 'Could not find module Test.QuickCheck' at Properties.hs:20. My own successful build only worked because this specific development machine already had QuickCheck 2.18.0.0 registered in ~/.cabal/store and exposed via ~/.ghc/aarch64-darwin-9.14.1/environments/default -- state that is not part of the repository and is documented nowhere. A referee on a fresh GHC install following the paper's instructions literally would hit this error and have to independently discover and install QuickCheck.

Section 9 (lines ~1086-1088) states the code 'is bundled with the arXiv source of this paper as an ancillary directory and included in the supplementary materials.' I searched the full repository (README/Makefile/*ancillary*/*supplement* patterns, and the top-level tree) and found no ancillary directory, submission tarball, DOI, or archive of any kind; the code is reachable only via the shared project's src/lieb-robinson-locality/ path used during development. This specific distribution claim does not correspond to any artifact that exists.

Section 9 (line ~1100-1102) describes the velocity-fitting method as 'a least-squares fit to the contour c(x,t)=10^{-2}', i.e. a fixed absolute commutator-norm level of 0.01. The actual implementation does something different: Ising.hs's frontPosition thresholds each time-row at 10% of that same row's own maximum value (Main.hs calls `frontPosition 0.1 row`), a per-row relative threshold, not a fixed absolute contour. I confirmed by grepping the whole source tree that the literal constant 0.01 (or 1e-2) never appears anywhere in the Haskell code. These are not the same algorithm and are not guaranteed to produce the same fitted velocity on other parameter choices; the paper's prose does not describe the method that actually produced its own reported v_fit.

Section 9 (lines ~1108-1111) claims: 'The Properties module states QuickCheck properties that formalize the paper's elementary claims: ... the reweighting inequalities ||F_a||<=||F|| and C_{F_a}<=C_F of Lemma 2.4 ...'. Only the first inequality is actually tested: prop_reweightNorm (Properties.hs) checks ||F_a||<=||F|| via normFTrunc. The second, C_{F_a}<=C_F, is never tested anywhere -- Properties.hs never imports convFTrunc (confirmed by grep across the whole directory), and the only use of convFTrunc in the codebase is in Main.hs's demoFFunctions, which prints C_F and C_{F_a} for one fixed configuration (a=0.5, unit-distance pair, R=300 truncation) annotated with a comment '(Lemma 2.4: <= C_F)' for the reader to eyeball -- there is no boolean assertion, no randomized sweep, and no effect on the program's exit code or pass/fail count. Running the program, I confirmed the two printed numbers do satisfy the inequality on this one configuration (2.1640 <= 2.6971), but that is a single manual data point, not the randomized QuickCheck property the paper's own text says exists. Of the two inequalities Section 9 explicitly names as QuickCheck-verified, only half actually is.

Minor: the chain length (N=7, open boundary) is sampled out to t=1.8 (Main.hs's `times` list), and the printed commutator-norm table shows the near-boundary site x=0 already declining from its peak (1.95 at t=0.9 to 0.73 at t=1.8) while the far end (x=6) is only just becoming non-negligible (0.02) -- consistent with the wavefront beginning to interact with the small chain's finite size by the last sample times. This does not invalidate the demonstration -- the paper's own Remark in Section 9 explicitly disclaims any infinite-volume claim -- but the specific quoted v_fit carries some finite-size/threshold/boundary sensitivity inherent to a 7-site exact-diagonalization demo that the paper does not discuss.
